topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure lay open on the coffee table, the map of the new city a vibrant, alien landscape. Sarah traced the outline of a distant skyline with a hesitant finger, the cheap paper crinkling under her touch. Liam’s packed boxes were stacked in the hallway, a silent testament to a future that was rapidly dissolving into impossibility. Every time she glanced at his worn denim jacket hanging by the door, a sharp, cold clench tightened in her chest. She felt the air in the apartment grow thin, suffocating. "So, you're actually doing this?" she managed, the sound of her own voice surprisingly brittle. Liam just offered a smile that didn't quite reach his eyes, a practiced, easygoing gesture that felt like a deliberate distance. She stood up, needing to move, needing to disrupt the stillness, but her legs felt heavy, rooted to the spot as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the casual way he mentioned the lease signing, the way he brushed off the reality of the distance with a shrug. Now, staring at the empty space where his favorite armchair used to sit, the silence of the room was deafening. My hands started shaking, a fine tremor that made gripping my mug feel like a monumental effort. I paced the length of the living room three times, the worn rug yielding slightly beneath my bare feet. Each step was a desperate attempt to physically escape the confines of this shared space, but the walls seemed to lean in, pressing the air out of my lungs. When he finally looked up, his expression was apologetic, but that apology only amplified the sheer weight of the impending separation. "It's just... a big change, Sarah," he murmured, his voice pitched too high, and I could only nod, unable to formulate a response that didn't sound like a plea.

[story 3]

The sunlight streaming through the window felt harsh, illuminating every dust mote dancing in the air, mocking the inertia holding me in place. I was meticulously cleaning the bookshelf, smoothing the spines of old novels until the wood gleamed, a pointless ritual against the churning anxiety in my gut. It wasn't just the physical space; it was the blueprint of our shared existence being redrawn, erasing familiar landmarks. I stopped wiping, leaning my forehead against the cool wood of a volume, trying to anchor myself to something solid. The knowledge that soon, his calls would be different—longer, punctuated by time zones and unspoken farewells—made my throat constrict. I clenched my jaw so hard my teeth ached, wishing the floor would simply open up beneath me and swallow me whole, offering a sudden, complete void instead of this unbearable pressure.
